content.text:
*"The Lord looks down from heaven on all mankind to see 
if there are any who understand, any who seek God."*{Psalm 14:2}{The introduction of quantifiers marks the beginning of modern logic.}{Gottlob Frege}

Propositions allow us to express specific true-or-false statements, but 
mathematics often requires statements about *all* or *some* 
members of a set. For this, we need predicates and quantifiers.

content.title: Predicate
content.content:
A **predicate** (or propositional function) is a 
statement containing one or more variables that becomes a proposition 
when specific values are substituted for the variables.

We write $P(x)$ to denote a predicate $P$ with variable $x$, and $P(x, y)$ 
for a predicate with two variables.

content.title: Predicates
content.problem:
Let $P(x)$ be "$x > 5$" with domain $\R$.

- $P(3)$ is the proposition "$3 > 5$," which is false.

- $P(10)$ is the proposition "$10 > 5$," which is true.

- $P(5)$ is the proposition "$5 > 5$," which is false.

Let $Q(x, y)$ be "$x + y = 10$" with domain $\Z$.

- $Q(3, 7)$ is "$3 + 7 = 10$," which is true.

- $Q(4, 4)$ is "$4 + 4 = 10$," which is false.

content.title: Universal Quantifier
content.content:
The **universal quantifier** $\forall$ 
(read "for all") asserts that a predicate is true for every element in 
its domain.

The statement $\forall x\, P(x)$ means "For all $x$, $P(x)$ is true."

content.title: Universal quantification
content.problem:
Let the domain be all real numbers.

- $\forall x\, (x + 0 = x)$ — "Every real number plus zero equals itself." (True)

- $\forall x\, (x^2 \geq 0)$ — "Every real number squared is non-negative." (True)

- $\forall x\, (x > 0)$ — "Every real number is positive." (False; consider $x = -1$)

content.text:
To prove $\forall x\, P(x)$ is **true**, you must show $P(x)$ holds 
for *every* $x$ in the domain. To prove it is **false**, you 
need only find one **counterexample**—a 
specific $x$ for which $P(x)$ is false.

content.title: Existential Quantifier
content.content:
The **existential quantifier** $\exists$ 
(read "there exists") asserts that a predicate is true for at least one 
element in its domain.

The statement $\exists x\, P(x)$ means "There exists an $x$ such that 
$P(x)$ is true."

content.title: Existential quantification
content.problem:
Let the domain be all integers.

- $\exists x\, (x^2 = 4)$ — "There exists an integer whose square is 4." (True; $x = 2$ or $x = -2$)

- $\exists x\, (x + 5 = x)$ — "There exists an integer $x$ with $x + 5 = x$." (False)

- $\exists x\, (x > 1000000)$ — "There exists an integer greater than one million." (True)

content.text:
To prove $\exists x\, P(x)$ is **true**, you need only exhibit one 
**witness**—a specific $x$ for which $P(x)$ is true. To prove it is 
**false**, you must show $P(x)$ fails for *every* $x$.

content.title: De Morgan's Laws for Quantifiers
content.content:
$$
\lnot(\forall x\, P(x)) &\equiv \exists x\, \lnot P(x) \\
\lnot(\exists x\, P(x)) &\equiv \forall x\, \lnot P(x)
$$
content.label: Theorem

items: Denying "all swans are white" is asserting "some swan is not white."
Denying "some number is negative" is asserting "all numbers are non-negative."

content.title: Negating quantified statements
content.problem: Negate the statement: "Every student passed the exam."
content.solution:
Let $P(x)$ be "$x$ passed the exam" with domain all students.

The original statement is $\forall x\, P(x)$.

Its negation is $\exists x\, \lnot P(x)$: "Some student did not pass the 
exam" (or equivalently, "At least one student failed").

content.text: Many mathematical statements involve multiple quantifiers. The order matters!

content.title: Order of quantifiers
content.problem:
Let the domain be real numbers. Consider:

1. $\forall x\, \exists y\, (x + y = 0)$ — "For every $x$, there exists a $y$ such that $x + y = 0$."
 
 This is **true**: given any $x$, we can choose $y = -x$.

2. $\exists y\, \forall x\, (x + y = 0)$ — "There exists a $y$ such that for all $x$, $x + y = 0$."
 
 This is **false**: no single $y$ works for all $x$.

Switching the order of $\forall$ and $\exists$ changes the meaning!

content.title: Reading Nested Quantifiers
content.content:
Read from left to right. Each quantifier's variable can depend on variables 
quantified to its left, but not to its right. In $\forall x\, \exists y\, 
P(x,y)$, the choice of $y$ can depend on $x$. In $\exists y\, \forall x\, 
P(x,y)$, $y$ must be chosen before knowing $x$.

marginNotes.text:
I the Lord search the heart and examine 
the mind, to reward each person according to their conduct.

For my thoughts are not your thoughts, 
neither are your ways my ways, declares the Lord.
